subject: Surgery
title: Bowel surgery
preambleMarkdown:
# Bowel surgery



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Bowel surgery is a procedure or practical clinical workflow in surgery.
- This article focuses on indications, anatomy, perioperative risk, operative options, postoperative care, and complications.
- The practical clinical priority is safe performance, correct indication, and complication prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Bowel surgery to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Bowel surgery is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is safe performance, correct indication, and complication prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnostic, therapeutic, emergent, urgent, or elective use.
- Bedside, image-guided, endoscopic, operative, or hybrid approach.
- Low-risk versus high-risk patient based on bleeding, infection, airway, anatomy, anticoagulation, and comorbid disease.
- Initial procedure, repeat procedure, failed procedure, or complication-management setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Bowel surgery depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In surgery, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Bowel surgery.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Bowel surgery disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Bowel surgery should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Bowel surgery first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Bowel surgery, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The clinical presentation is the problem that makes the procedure necessary.
- Assess severity, stability, anatomy, bleeding risk, infection risk, allergies, medications, and prior procedures.
- The most important history is what changes indication, urgency, consent, sedation, or aftercare.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Bowel surgery, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused surgery examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Bowel surgery presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Bowel surgery with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Bowel surgery is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Bowel surgery should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm indication, alternatives, risks, consent, equipment, positioning, monitoring, and post-procedure plan.
- Correct modifiable risks when time allows, especially anticoagulation, coagulopathy, infection, and unstable physiology.
- Stop or escalate if anatomy is unclear, complications develop, or the intended endpoint is not reached.

### Initial Management

- Confirm the indication, urgency, consent pathway, patient identity, site, equipment, and monitoring plan for Bowel surgery.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses safe performance, correct indication, and complication prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Perform the procedure only when the indication, anatomy, equipment, operator skill, and rescue plan are acceptable.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Bowel surgery, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Bowel surgery may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Bowel surgery patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Bowel surgery, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Bowel surgery in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Bowel surgery, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Bowel surgery does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Bowel surgery may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Bowel surgery, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Bowel surgery as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the indication, contraindication, key anatomy, major complication, and aftercare.
- Document neurovascular status, bleeding risk, consent, and response when relevant.
- Never let the desire to complete a procedure override patient stability or unsafe anatomy.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Bowel surgery draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Diseases of the Small Bowel

_Source lines: 6134-6833._

```text
DISEASES OF THE SMALL BOWEL I	N	O	O	O	O	O			U	U	Fi	Fl	Y
Crohn Disease
I. Epidemiology
A. Incidence: 4 cases per 1 00 ,000 per year
B. Prevalence: 40 to 1 6 0 cases per 100 ,000 persons
C. More common in North America and Europe
l . Slightly more common in women
2. Age of onset has bimodal peak: one peak at 15 to 25 years and another at
55 to 65 years
3. In the United States, more common among whites, and three to four times
more common among ethnic Jews. More common in persons of higher
socioeconomic status.

II. Pathogenesis and Pathology. Many theories have been advanced, but none have
been validated. An altered immune response may be involved, although no specific
defect has been identified.

A. Features of Crohn disease are present anywhere from the mouth to the anus. The
most common location is ileocolic (60% ) .

B. The disease tends t o be discontinuous and segmental.

C. Transmural lymphocytic inflammation with noncaseating granulomata is the
classic pathology in the bowel. The gross appearance of involved bowel is thick
ened mesentery with enlarged friable lymph nodes and "fat wrapping" of bowel.

Ill. Clinical Presentation
A. Extraintestinal manifestations of Crohn disease may antedate the onset of the
intestinal changes by months to years.

l . One-third of patients demonstrate perianal fistulae .

2. O ther Undings include oral aphthous ulceration, rashes such as ery
thema nodosum and pyoderma gangrenosum, uveitis, polyarthralgias or
monoarthralgias, and sclerosing cholangitis.

B. Symptoms of Crohn disease generally relate to bowel wall strictures. Charac
teristic complaints include bloating, abdominal pain, abdominal distention, and
postobstructive diarrhea.

l . Active disease or multiple strictures can result in a "failure to thrive" catabolic
state.

2. Diarrhea may indicate Hstula of the proximal bowel to colon.

C. Bowel perforation and clinically evident bleeding are not frequently seen.

D. Other symptoms may result from fistulae to pelvic organs , such as bladder, or to
skin, or retroperitoneal fibrosis causing ureteral obstruction.

E. Severe involvement of the terminal ileum leads to gallstone formation and macro
cytic anemia due to loss of absorption of bile salts and vitamin B12.

IV. Diagnosis
A. Standard barium small bowel follow-through (or enteroclysis) is very helpful.

It shows mucosal ulceration and nodularity, wall thickening, strictures, and
Ustulae .

B. Colonoscopy i s useful i n colonic Crohn disease, and intubation of the terminal
ileum may confirm ileocolic disease. Biopsies can also be taken to conHrm a
pathologic diagnosis.

C. Computed tomography (CT) scanning is not as speciHc as small bowel
follow-through but may better demonstrate complications of the disease
(e.g. , obstruction, abscesses, or fistulae) .

V. Treatment-Medical
A. Antibiotics are of little value except in anorectal disease where metronidazole has
shown some effectiveness.

B. Mildly symptomatic disease should be treated with 5-aminosalicylic acid,
especially ileocolic and colonic variants.

C. Moderately active disease mandates use of immunosuppressives.

l. First-line therapy is corticosteroids , which are weaned quickly, if possible, to
avoid the signiHcant adverse effects of steroid administration.

2 . Steroid-resistant patients are offered azidothymidine or 6-mercaptopurine.

a. Suppress helper T -cell and natural killer cell activity
b. Cause myelosuppression, hepatotoxicity, and pancreatitis
D. Failure of immunosuppressive therapy may require a short course of an antitu
mor necrosis factor agent (infliximab) .

l . Chimeric human: mouse monoclonal antibody
2. Primary indication for use is fistula healing.

## 3 . Long-term use is not advisable because of concerns about induction of non

Hodgkin lymphoma.

a. Medical management should be continued for only as long as the patient
with Crohn disease is symptomatic.

b. There is no evidence for the prophylactic use of immunosuppressives to
prevent recurrence of disease.

VI. Treatment-Surgical
A. General principles
l . Up to 90% of patients with Crohn disease eventually need surgery.

2. Crohn disease is the second leading cause of surgically induced short bowel
syndrome in adults , and preserving bowel length is critical in surgery for
Crohn disease.

## 3. Guidelines

a. Do not look for normal bowel to use for anastomoses. Use thickened or fat
wrapped bowel, as long as it can be safely sutured or stapled.

b . Do not use frozen sections to look for uninvolved margins. You will only
waste usable bowel.

c. Leave the enlarged nodes alone unless you think you are dealing with a
Crohn disease-associated cancer. Resection of the mesentery causes devas
cularization of uninvolved bowel and further loss of bowel length.

d. Avoid bypass of involved segments because this leaves active disease in
place, causing symptoms and extraintestinal manifestations to continue.

4. Laparoscopic approaches to Crohn disease have been shown to be safe and
effective, especially at the primary operation.

5. Long side-to-side anastomoses are favored over end-to-end.

B. Indications for surgery
l . Absolute indications include perforation, hemorrhage, suspicion of cancer, and
nonresolving bowel obstruction.

## 2 . Relative indications are symptomatic fistula (e.g. , enterovesical Hstula with

recurrent infections) , abscess , and "failure to thrive . "

## 3 . Resecting the intestinal disease has little impact o n most of the extraintestinal

manifestations of Crohn disease, and therefore, resection should not be pursued.

C. Operative strategy in Crohn disease
l . To save bowel length in small bowel disease, strictureplasty is favored over
resection unless the strictures are all located in a short segment of bowel.

a. Strictures less than 5 em are best dealt with by the Heineke-Mikulicz
approach of converting a longitudinal enterotomy to a transverse closure.

b . Strictures up to 1 0 em may require a Finney procedure .

c. Longer strictures can be opened with a sliding type isoperistaltic
strictureplasty.

2. Ileocolic disease is generally resected with standard right hemicolectomy,
creating a long side-to-side stapled ileocolostomy.

## 3 . Although segmental resection of colonic Crohn is generally pursued, some

centers have reported much longer times to disease recurrence by using total
abdominal colectomy if the rectum is uninvolved or even total proctocolec
tomy with end ileostomy creation.

4. Crohn bowel should not knowingly be used for the creation of pouches , such
as the ]-pouch for the ileoanal pull-through procedure .

VII. Cancer Risk
A. Risk of adenocarcinoma development in the Crohn segment is estimated at 15 to
100 times the risk of small bowel adenocarcinoma.

l. The likelihood of malignant transformation begins after l O years of active disease.

If cancer develops, it is generally after 20 to 30 years of carrying the diagnosis.

2. Experts are uncertain about whether patients with more symptomatic Crohn
disease have a greater likelihood of undergoing malignant transformation than
those with less symptomatic disease.

B. The prognosis of Crohn-associated small bowel cancer is worse because of its late
discovery (symptoms of Crohn disease mimic those of a cancer) , not inherent
biologic aggressiveness.

Small Bowel Obstruction
I. Pathogenesis: Causes of Mechanical Obstruction
A. Foreign body
l . Can be anything ingested: coins, batteries, undigested plant or food material, hair
2. Treatment: Laxatives may help with a partial obstruction; complete obstruc
tion usually requires surgical removal.

B. Gallstone ileus
l . Generally seen in the elderly and debilitated
2. Symptoms of "tumbling obstruction" : pattern of sequential, spontaneously
resolving obstructions as gallstone alternately becomes lodged and then passes
more distal in small bowel
3. Cause is usually chronic cholecystitis with fistula between gallbladder and
second portion of duodenum, through which a large gallstone gains entry into
the GI tract.

4. Stone eventually impacts itself in narrow terminal ileum, where complete
bowel obstruction results.

## 5 . Abdominal films show high-grade distal small bowel obstruction, calcified gall

stone outside the right upper quadrant (possibly) , and pneumobilia.

6. Treatment is to first rehydrate patient due to persistent vomiting. At explora
tion, milk the stone back into the dilated bowel and extract through the en
terotomy. Make sure there are no other more proximal stones and close the
enterotomy. Due to tenuous patient condition, cholecystectomy and fistula
closure are usually not pursued in the acute setting.

C. Intussusception
l . Five percent to 1 5 % of cases occur in adults, representing less than 1% of
adult bowel obstruction.

2. Ninety percent of cases are associated with a lead point such as a neoplasm
(benign or malignant) , diverticulum, old suture line, or adhesion.

3. Diagnosis usually made by contrast study or CT scan
4. Barium column decompression, useful in children, is rarely used in adults .

a. Presence of lead point makes radiologic reduction unlikely to be successful.

b . Association with malignancy mandates surgery in most cases.

c. The increasing risk of perforation and resultant malignant seeding of perito
neal cavity makes this practice questionable .

## 5 . Surgery should be undertaken to decompress the intussusception and evaluate

for cause.

D. Malignant obstruction
l . This condition rarely resolves with nonoperative measures.

2 . A short trial of decompression may succeed. If this fails, resect or bypass.

## 3 . If the obstruction cannot be reduced surgically, somatostatin may reduce

cramping pain for palliation.

E . Crohn disease: trial of steroids or other immunosuppressants before exploration
F. Radiation enteritis
l . Always consider in patients with history of radiation for gynecologic
malignancy. It is less common after radiation therapy for colon or prostate
cancer.

2 . It usually occurs less than 1 0 years after radiation therapy.

3. Signs include obliterative vasculitis and muscular Hbrosis.

4. Surgery is almost always needed and can be daunting due to pelvic Hbrosis.

In some cases, bypass with venting stomas at the ends of the trapped pelvic
bowel segment is all that is possible.

G. Adhesions
l. More than half of all small bowel obstructions are adhesions in patients with
previous surgery.

2. The vast maj ority of these occur post abdominal surgery, but occasionally
congenital bands are encountered.

3. Up to 80% of adhesive small bowel obstructions resolve with nasogastric
decompression alone.

H. Hernias
l . Hernias are the second most common cause of obstruction in Western coun
tries and first in the Third World.

2. In the United States, due to high rate of elective repair of inguinal and umbili
cal hernias, rare hernias such as femoral, obturator, bowel entrapment in the
foramen of Winslow, or congenital defects of the omentum should be consid
ered as causes of adhesions.

## 3 . Also, consider the very dangerous Richter hernia, where only a portion of the

bowel wall is fixed in the hernia sac, leading to a partial obstruction picture
with high risk of bowel perforation.

I . Volvulus
l . Loop of bowel is twisted more than 1 80 degrees about its axis and is caused by
mismatch of bowel length to narrow area of attachment.

2. Ileocolic most frequent in small bowel but can occur in small bowel alone
3. Endoscopic decompression is generally not useful (unlike sigmoid volvulus) .

4. Use laparotomy to decompress volvulus and either perform cecopexy or, pref
erably, resect the involved segment.

II. Functional Obstruction: Ileus. Motility alters without evidence of luminal
obstruction.

A. Causes
l. Neurogenic (e.g. , spinal cord injury)
2. Retroperitoneal process or surgery (e.g. , ureteral colic)

## 3. Opiates

4. Metabolic abnormalities, including hypokalemia, uremia, calcium or magne
sium imbalance, hypothyroidism, and hyperglycemia
5. Intra-abdominal or distant infection
6. Drugs such as anticholinergics and antihistamines
B. Normal return of small bowel function after abdominal surgery is 6 to 24 hours .

C. Ileus is less common after laparoscopic procedures compared with open
surgeries.

Ill. Diagnosis of Small Bowel Obstruction
A. No reliable physical finding or serum laboratory test distinguishes ileus Xom
mechanical obstruction or rules out strangulated obstruction.

B. History is critical.

1. Prior abdominal surgery
2. Signs of infection or peritonitis
3. Prior history of obstructions

4. History of malignancy or inflammatory bowel disease
5. History of abdominal trauma
a. Splenosis
b. Diaphragmatic hernia
6. History of endometriosis
C. Radiology of obstruction
l . Plain films (three-way abdomen) : said to be diagnostic in up to 80% of
patients
a. Air-fluid levels may be present in both ileus and mechanical obstruction,
although they are more common with latter.

b. Assess for free air.

2. Contrast studies
a. These studies may better delineate both nature and level of obstruction.

b . Generally, these use water-soluble contrast unless closed loop obstruction
is suspected. The hydrophilic activity of water-soluble contrast may cause
swelling and perforation of the closed loop.

3. CT scanning: Most would consider this the preferred test for evaluating small
bowel obstruction.

a. Similar efHcacy to contrast studies in terms of luminal findings
b. More sensitive to secondary signs in obstruction such as presence of
malignancy, mesenteric stranding , abscess, vascular compromise, intes
tinal wall thickening, and pneumatosis (air in the wall of the intestines)
(Fig. 6- l ) .

IV. Treatment o f Small Bowel Obstruction
A. Obstructions with low likelihood of resolution with "conservative" measures
such as malignancy-associated obstructions, high-grade obstructions, and hernias
should be operated on promptly after fluid resuscitation.

B. If an ileus, assess for inciting factors (especially opiate use if fresh postoperative
patient) and correct these if possible.

l . CT is particularly helpful if the cause is uncertain because it conHrms the
absence or presence of the transition zone associated with a mechanical
obstruction and allows assessment for other intra-abdominal processes, such
as abscess.

2. It may be necessary to try to administer an epidural with continuous
local anesthesia (not opiates) to allow cessation of intravenous (IV)
opiates .

## 3 . There is some evidence for vagal stimulation through limited oral intake or

gum chewing in order to reduce the duration of ileus.

C. Despite the adage that "the sun should not set on a bowel obstruction," what
to do with the lower risk obstructions, such as the early postoperative adhesive
obstruction, remains problematic.

l. Most surgeons give a 24- to 48-hour "expectant" trial of nasogastric decom
pression and bowel rest with close observation of the patient's status and
abdominal examination.

## 2 . There is some evidence for using the time it takes for oral contrast to enter

the colon as a predictor of the likelihood of resolution of partial small bowel
obstructions.

Meckel Diverticulum
I. Epidemiology and Etiology
A. Meckel diverticulum is the most common congenital abnormality of the GI
tract.

B. It is found in l % to 2% of the population, with slightly higher male incidence.

More than 50% of symptomatic cases present in male children younger than
10 years of age. Complications occur disproportionately in males.

C. On average, the location is 60 em proximal to the ileocecal valve , on the
antimesenteric aspect.

D. Factors predictive of symptomatic Meckel diverticulum
l . Male sex (cause of male predominance unknown)
2. Diverticular length less than 2 em (increased length : orifice ratio inclines
toward development of diverticulitis)
3. Presence of ectopic tissue-gastric or pancreatic
4. Presence of mesodiverticular or vitelloumbilical band
E. It results from failure of the omphalomesenteric (vitelline) duct to close. This
should occur around the ninth gestational week.

F . Complete failure to involute results in the infrequently encountered enteroum
bilical fistula
II. Pathology
A. Meckel diverticulum is a true diverticulum of all layers of the intestinal wall.

B. Up to one-third of Meckel diverticula contain heterotropic tissue , with gastric
and pancreatic the most frequently found.

C . The majority of symptomatic diverticula are lined with gastric mucosa.

Ill. Clinical Presentation
A. In children, bleeding from Meckel diverticula is usually occult, manifesting as
unexplained anemia , although more brisk hemorrhage presenting as melena
or even hematochezia has been seen. Bleeding due to peptic ulceration is
the most common presentation, with occasional cases of peptic-induced
perforation.

B. In adults, bowel obstruction is most frequent, caused by one of several
mechanisms :
l . Diverticulitis causing Hbrous stricture
2. Ectopic tissue , usually pancreatic, or the diverticulum itself serving as lead
point for intussusception
3. Internal herniation under a mesodiverticular band (remnant of the duct
attaching the root of mesentery to diverticulum)
4. Volvulus of ileum around vitelloumbilical band (bowel suspended from the
base of the umbilicus)
C. Obstructive complaints may be partial intermittent type, especially with volvulus,
or acute complete small bowel obstruction, as with intussusception.

IV. Diagnostic Workup
A. Rarely diagnosed preoperatively
B. Acute appendicitis is the most common preoperative diagnosis . Meckel diverticu
litis is clinically indistinguishable from acute appendicitis.

C. CT scan and ultrasound have low sensitivity in this setting.

D. Barium small-bowel series is more sensitive but generally is not used in the
presence of acute abdomen because of concern for perforation.

E. Technetium scan is widely reported to have the highest sensitivity but detects
only Meckel diverticula that contain gastric mucosa.

V. Surgical Treatment
A. In the acute setting, limited resection of the ileal segment containing the diver
ticulum with primary anastomosis is indicated.

B. Controversy persists regarding indications for resection of the incidentally
discovered Meckel diverticulum.

l. All incidentally discovered Meckel diverticula in children should be resected.

2. Some advocate the same strategy for adults, whereas others argue that one
would have to resect almost l ,000 diverticula to save the life of one adult who
would subsequently present with complications of the disease.

## 3 . Factors favoring resection of the asymptomatic Meckel diverticula are palpable

ectopic tissue (generally pancreatic, not gastric) , diverticular length less than
2 em, and the presence of an associated band.

a. Vitelloumbilical bands can simply be divided without diverticulectomy.

b. Mesodiverticular bands cannot be divided because they are often the sole
blood supply to the diverticulum; division of these bands necessitates con
comitant diverticulectomy.

C. No incidental diverticulectomy should be pursued in the presence of peritonitis
or in the medically unstable patient.

Other Small Bowel Diverticula
I. Epidemiology
A. Eighty percent of small bowel diverticula are in the duodenum; the rest are
j ejunoileal.

B. Most occur within 2 em of the ampulla of Vater.

II. Pathogenesis
A. True cause is unknown but likely secondary to abnormal pulsion/peristalsis
leading to increased intraluminal pressures (intestinal akinesis) .

B. Congenital diverticula are associated with duodenal atresia .

C. Occur at defects in the bowel wall at entrance of large vessels
D. Emerge on the mesenteric boarder of the small bowel
E. Most diverticula are extraluminal.

Ill. Clinical Presentation
A. Majority are asymptomatic.

l. They are most commonly found at autopsy or on barium swallow.

2. Jejunoileal diverticular are more commonly symptomatic .

B. Can present as small bowel diverticulitis and/or small bowel perforation
C. Chronic iron deficiency anemia secondary to long-standing GI bleeding
D. Biliary/pancreatic duct obstruction from diverticula blocking the ampulla of
Vater
E. NonspeciHc epigastric pain
IV. Diagnosis
A. Lab tests are nonspecific and of limited value.

l. Complete blood count (CBC) revealing a leukocytosis from diverticulitis/
perforation or iron deficiency anemia from a long-standing GI bleed
2. Elevated amylase/lipase anor hyperbilirubinemia Xom obstruction of the
ampulla of Vater
B. Imaging
l . Diverticula perforation presents as Xee air under the diaphragm on
chest/abdominal X-ray.

2. CT scan: phlegmon/abscess, free air from diverticulitis or perforation
3. Barium swallow/upper GI series
4. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) and endoscopic retrograde cholangio
pancreatography (ERCP) can endoscopically visualize the diverticula.

V. Treatment
A. Based on severity of complications
B. Conservative medical management
l . Nothing by mouth (NPO) , maintenance intravenous fluids (MIVFs) , broad
spectrum antibiotics in the setting of diverticulitis
C. Surgical management not indicated unless perforation of uncontrolled GI bleed
occurs

## 1 . Diverticulectomy

2. Exploratory laparotomy with small bowel resection
Benign Small Bowel Tumors
I. General Principles
A. These tumors represent about one-third of all small bowel neoplasms.

B. Growth pattern predicts associated symptoms and signs.

l . Infiltrative: bleeding
2. Intraluminal (e.g. , polypoid) : obstruction either due to lesion or as lead point
for intussusception; low-level bleeding
3. Serosal: pivot point for small bowel volvulus
C. Tend to be slow-growing with long duration, protean symptomatology
II. Hyperplastic Polyps
A. Predilection for distal duodenum and small bowel
B. No malignant potential
C. Rarely symptomatic, except as lead point of intussusception
Ill. Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumor
A. Gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) is the most common symptomatic small
bowel lesion.

B. Benign leiomyomata tend to be smaller in size and Hrmer than GIST .

C. Differentiating leiomyoma from GIST is difficult but is suggested by lack of cellu
larity on light microscopy, fewer than two mitoses per 10 high-power Helds, and
absence of CD34 and CD l l 7 staining on immunohistochemistry.

D. GIST presents with partial obstructive symptoms and minor bleeding Xom ero
sion of overlying mucosa.

E. Because intraoperative differentiation between leiomyoma and malignant GIST is
difficult, treatment of all smooth muscle tumors of the small bowel is resection,
with at least a 2-cm margin in all directions, and avoidance of tumor spillage or
rupture.

IV. Lipoma
A. Benign submucosal tumors found anywhere in small bowel
B. If large, may cause bowel obstruction and/or intussusception
C. Occasional bleeding from mucosal erosion
D. Require resection if symptomatic and are highly amenable to laparoscopic
resection
V. Hemangiomata
A. These may comprise up to 1 0% of all small bowel lesions.

B. The cavernous variant is the most common.

C. They are rarely large enough to cause obstruction unless intramural hematoma
develops.

D. Bleeding, occasionally massive, is the most common presentation, followed by
finding as lead point of intussusception.

E. They may be difficult to localize, even using arteriography. Intraoperative enter

F. Arteriographic embolization can be tried for significant bleeding; otherwise, use
surgical resection.

VI. Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome
A. This autosomal dominant disorder (mutation of 1 9p2.3 [LKB 1 ] gene) is charac-
terized by mucocutaneous pigmentation and myriad benign Gl hamartomas.

B. Ninety percent of affected individuals have small bowel hamartomas.

C. No nuclear atypia are seen in polyps.

D . Hamartomas can cause abdominal pain, Gl bleeding, and obstruction from
intussusception.

E. There is an increased rate of Gl cancers, but not from hamartomas. These arise in
synchronous adenomatous lesions .

F. Surveillance recommendations include the following: starting at age 1 0 years,
biannual esophagogastroduodenoscopy, colonoscopy, and barium small bowel
follow-through due to cancer risk.

G . Multiplicity of polyps makes localization of source of symptoms dicult
(Table 6-2) .
```

### Source Excerpt: Complications Associated with Common Small Bowel Surgeries

```text
COM PLICATIONS ASSOCIATED WITH COM MON SMALL
BOWEL SURG ERIES
I. Ileostomy
A. Loop ileostomy
l . Decompressed small bowel but incomplete diversion because contents can
enter distal limb
2. Can be used to protect a more distal anastomosis
3. Provides for simple closure without having to undergo laparotomy

B. End ileostomy
l . Complete diversion because no distal limb is present
2. Also functions to decompress the small bowel or to protect a more distal
anastomosis
3. Commonly requires laparotomy/difficult closure for reversal
C. Complications
l . Complications from ileostomies can include skin irritation, fluid loss, and elec
trolyte imbalance.

## 2 . Other complications of ostomies include infection, stomal prolapse/retraction,

inability to reverse , small bowel obstruction, peristomal hernia , stomal revi
sion, stricture, fistula, and bleeding/hemorrhage.

D . High ostomy output
l. Causes
a. Intra-abdominal sepsis, obstruction, short gut syndrome, high liquid oral intake

## 2. Pathophysiology

a. Small bowel cannot absorb NaCl and therefore water.

b. Fifty percent stop spontaneously whenever the proximal bowel hypertro
phies to compensate.

## 3 . Treatment

a. Medical treatment is directed at slowing the transit time of the small
bowel with proton pump inhibitors (PPis) or histamine-2 (H2) blockers,
Imodium, Lomotil, octreotide
b. May require IV hydration if output is greater than a liter a day and results
in dehydration
II. Short Gut Syndrome SGS1
A. Commonly occurs whenever you have < 200 em of removal or > 50% loss of
native small bowel
l . Minimal length required to avoid SGS: 60 em of small bowel or 45 em with an
intact ileocecal valve
2. Ileum adapts better than the j ejunum.

B. Causes
l. Crohn disease
2. Extensive surgical resection: This may occur with one operation or after
several operations.

3. Radiation, vascular insufficiency

## 4. Trauma

5. Recurrent obstruction
6. Necrotizing enterocolitis (NE C; pediatric)
7. Intestinal atresia (pediatric)
8. Volvulus (pediatric)
C. Pathophysiology
l . Loss of GI absorptive surface
2. Adaption may take greater than a year for an adult and greater than 4 years for
a child.

## 3 . At risk for kidney stones, gallstones, and peptic ulcer disease due to high gas

trin levels : may consider prophylactic cholecystectomy
D. Diagnosis

## 1 . Clinical diagnosis

2. Normal ostomy output should be 10 to 15 mLffkglhr, usually much higher
with short gut.

E. Treatment
l . Most cases will resolve on their own whenever the small bowel hypertrophies
to compensate.

2. Total parenteral nutrition for nutritional support
3. Low residue/elemental diet
4. PPis/H2 blockers
5. Antiperistalsis agents

6. Vitamin supplementation
7. Surgical treatments include stricturoplasty, tapering enteroplasty, artificial
```

```text
SMALL BOWEL TRAUMA
I. Epidemiology
A. Twenty percent to 30% blunt versus 70% to 80% penetrating
B. Jejunum is most commonly injured.

C. Blunt

## 1 . Bowel contusion

2. Mesenteric contusion
3. Serosal tears

## 4. Perforations

D. Penetrating

## 1 . Most commonly cause serosal tears and perforation

E. Small bowel injury scale (Table 6-3)
II. Clinical Presentation
A. Seatbelt sign, abdominal pain, peritonitis , shock
Ill. Diagnosis
A. Clinical diagnosis: based on history and physical exam
B. Plain film X-ray: Look for free peritoneal air.

C. FAST exam : low sensitivity and speciHcity for hallow viscus injury
D . Diagnostic peritoneal lavage: The presence of white blood cells greater than
500 cells per high-powered field, gram stain positive bilirubin, or amylase may be
more indicative of hallow viscus injury.

E. CT scan: Look for free peritoneal air, bowel wall thickening, or triangles of free
fluid within the bowel loops (Figs. 6-2 and 6-3 ) .

F . Exploration with laparotomy o r laparoscopy will provide definitive diagnosis and
treatment.

IV. Treatment
A. Indications for surgery

## 1 . Peritonitis

## 2. Uncontrolled hemorrhage (Fig. 6-4)

3. Prolong symptoms of abdominal pain
B. Surgical management

## 1 . Exploratory laparotomy or laparoscopy

a. Small bowel resection
b. Possibility of a second look operation if leaving bowel in discontinuity or if
ischemia is a possibility
```
